title: [source] Only adjustments attached to logs whose status is exactly "done" and whose times

given: An asset has a done increment of 5 timestamped after the current/requested cutoff.
It also has a pending increment of 3 timestamped before the cutoff.
It has a done increment of 2 before the cutoff.
when: Inventory is calculated at that cutoff.
then: Only the done pre-cutoff increment contributes, so the result includes 2 but not 5 or 3.

why: A materializer could naturally apply every recorded adjustment, or regard future-dated done records as current; both are explicitly excluded.

title: [source] A reset is an ordered assignment, not an additive adjustment: calculation starts

given: An asset has increment +10 at timestamp 100.
then: The result is 3: the +10 before the latest reset timestamp is excluded, reset assigns 4, and the later decrement is applied.

why: The adjustment type might be modeled as a normal event in an additive ledger, and selecting only one latest reset event would miss same-timestamp ordering behavior.

title: [source] A newly created, unsaved asset returns no inventory summaries at all, rather tha

given: An asset has not yet been persisted and is marked new.
when: Inventory is requested for the asset.
then: The result is an empty array.

why: A read model may initialize an inventory projection for every asset with zero values, but this API distinguishes a new asset from an existing asset with no adjustments.

title: [source] Events sharing the latest reset timestamp are retained and sequenced by ascendin

given: At timestamp 200, log ID 10 increments an asset by 3.
At timestamp 200, log ID 11 resets it to 4.
At timestamp 200, log ID 12 decrements it by 1.
then: All three same-timestamp events are processed by ID; the result is 3, because +3 is overwritten by reset 4 and then -1 is applied.

why: The reset cutoff is timestamp-inclusive rather than a strict event-position cutoff, so timestamp alone does not define the reset boundary.

title: [source] Inventory is calculated independently per exact (measure, units) pair; adjustmen

given: An asset has a mass increment in kilograms.
The same asset has a mass increment in pounds.
when: Inventory is requested for the asset with no units filter.
then: Two separate inventory summaries are returned, one per units ID; their values are not summed or converted.

why: A caller may treat an omitted filter or a measure-only request as a cross-unit total, but the implementation discovers distinct pairs and calculates each separately.
